textPlain: I see the bean counters are still in control at QCOM

I think its another symptom of the problem of there not being a clear way to get from your smart brainbox which you run a proper operating system on and do heavy computation on to driving lots of motors or similar. There are options, but there's not one that everyone defaults to and you can get good information on.
What problem are they solving for with the hybrid approach?
This is an example of what happens when your wallet is bigger than your imagination.

Weird take.  The Raspberry Pi Zero 2W would have seemed more like the natural comparison rather than the Raspberry Pi 4 or 5.I find it more interesting to think of the Arduino Uno Q as a Raspberry Pi Zero 2W but with 2GB / 4GB of RAM and even lower idle power than the other options with that much RAM.  That makes it intriguing as a very low power always-on headless server with GPIO as a bonus.

It's not exactly a big problem, but my company makes a three-axis desktop CNC machine powered by an ATMega328P that is dependent on having a Windows PC connected to it in order to run the proprietary software. Uno Q has me thinking we can eliminate the PC along with our custom PCB, making a more compact and convenient stand-alone system that would also come with a lower price tag. I may order a few to experiment with this idea, but again, it's not a huge problem and something only specific customers would care about.I'm curious what the Maker community comes up with, provided it sticks around and remains supported, unlike the Edison.
The problem of showing "synergy" between Arduino and Qualcomm.
RP Zero 2W is $15 and Uno Q is $44
